rationale_for_baseline_tier: A traumatised benign wart with controllable bleeding — needs prompt in-person haemostasis (a telehealth provider can't cauterise) and routine wart care, with an uncontrolled-bleeding/infection safety-net; not an emergency unless bleeding is uncontrolled.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Dressing soaked and blood dripping continuously despite 15 minutes of firm pressure, or the finger becomes hot/red/swollen with pus
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled bleeding or secondary infection needs in-person care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the dressing soaks through and keeps dripping despite 15 minutes of firm pressure, or the finger becomes hot, red and swollen with pus — go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single, rapidly-growing, smooth friable bleeding lump (unlike the warts)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: See a GP for assessment/possible biopsy
escalation_edges.rationale: Would suggest a pyogenic granuloma or other lesion needing removal/biopsy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a single smooth lump grows quickly and bleeds easily (different from the warts), see your GP for it to be checked.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reassure and give clear haemostasis instructions and a specific safety-net.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young child who bites/chews the warts
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise measures to stop biting and cover warts to limit spread.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: These are common warts. Press firmly to stop the bleeding (a clinician can seal it with a chemical stick), keep the dressing dry for 2 days, and the warts can be treated with wart paint or freezing. Stop your child biting them. Go to ED if the bleeding won't stop after 15 minutes of pressure or the finger gets infected.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day GP/nurse for haemostasis; GP in 2 weeks for wart treatment; ED for uncontrolled bleeding
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Firm pressure for bleeding; ED if it won't stop or the site is infected.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document traumatised verruca vulgaris (pyogenic granuloma screened negative), haemostasis, routine wart treatment options, and spread-prevention advice.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: No haemostasis advice or bleeding/infection safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing bleeding or a secondary infection is not recognised/managed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No in-person haemostasis or routine wart plan.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Emergency-ambulance escalation for a controllable bleeding benign wart.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot cauterise or dermoscope remotely; but multiplicity/chronicity/verrucous surface indicate benign warts.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Arrange in-person haemostasis + routine wart treatment with a bleeding/infection safety-net; biopsy only a solitary changing lesion.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Over-triaging as an aggressive tumour, or not controlling the bleeding.
